abstract.text: A Swift interface for (Open)LDAP.

inlineContent.text: Add the following dependency to your
The first thing you need is an
. There are two convenience static methods to get one:

code: // This uses the ldap:// scheme and the default LDAP port
// This uses the ldaps:// scheme and the default LDAPS port

inlineContent.text: Both methods optionally take a
parameter if you require a non-default port. If you need different schemes, there is the
initializer with which you can create a fully customized
Once you have a server, you need a connection.
has a method named
that will do exactly this for you:
With the connection you can then perform LDAP operations like e.g. binding:
The connection will automatically try to unbind itself once it’s deallocated, but you can also explicitly do so:

Note, however, that a connection cannot be re-bound once unbound. An unbound connection is invalid and a new connection has to be obtained from the server. Any operation on an unbound connection will fail (and assert in debug builds).
A connection can be duplicated. A normal duplicated connection depends on its original connection. This means that if a connection is invalidated (unbound), all its siblings will be invalid as well.
Duplicated connections will destroy themselves when deallocated - leaving the original connection valid. There is also an explicit
function which will destroy duplicated connections and unbind primary connections.
Note that a duplicate of an
retains its original connection to make sure the original connection doesn’t unbind itself during deallocation, thus rendering the duplicate invalid as well.
Ultimately, a connection can be used to search the directory. The
method is the way to go for that.
The first parameter is the type of object class you want to search for. It defines what kind of object will be returned. If you want any object, pass
in there. Note, however, that you won’t be able to extract much information from the resulting objects (see below for more information).
The
paremeter defines the base DN to use for the search.
With the optional
, wich defaults to
, you can further filter the results (in addition to the object class). Simply pass in a valid LDAP filter.

code: let results = try connection.search(for: ShadowAccount.self, inBase: "dc=mydomain,dc=com")

inlineContent.text: Object classes describe the attributes that are available on an object. In SwiftDirector, object classes are represented as protocols and (empty) structs which are used in combination with the
features of Swift to allow you to easily access information on an
(see below).
All object classes have to inherit from the
. However, since usually all object classes actually inherit from the top object class, it’s semantically more correct to let your object classes also inhert from
There is also a
struct, that will allow searching for any object class.

SwiftDirector currently makes no effort to deliver a conclusive list of object class implementations. The list of implementations that SwiftDirector ships will grow over time, though. It’s easy, however, to implement your own object class (or one that is missing). You simply create a protocol that is named after your object class and has the

suffix. The protocol itself simply defines the inheritance from other object classes but defines no requirements. In a protocol extension you then define the available attributes of this object class. Finally, you also add a struct that is named after your object class that can then be used for searching.
Let’s take a look at how a
object class (inheriting from
) might be implemented:

code: // Only make the attribute's type non-optional if LDAP requires the precense of the attribute!
// An optional attribute of type Int.
// Collections can always be declared non-optional. If the attribute is missing, the value will be an empty collection.
// The oid of the object class.
// The name as it occurs in e.g the objectClass attribute of an object
// An empty initializer allowing SwiftDirector to create an instance of your object class definition.

inlineContent.text: An

represents a concrete object retrieved from an LDAP directory. It’s a generic struct whose generic parameter has to be an object class. It gives access to the object class’ attributes which are exposed as simple properties thanks to the power of

code: let object: LDAPObject<ShadowAccount> // retrieved from a search
let username = object.userID // Extract the username

inlineContent.text: The

can also deal with the fact that in LDAP you can have an object that has multiple object classes (“multiple inheritance”). While you can’t directly access attributes of a simbling object class, there are methods that allow safe casting to different object classes:

code: let object: LDAPObject<ShadowAccount> // retrieved from a search
// Here you have access to attributes of the `InetOrgPerson` object class

inlineContent.text: You can also simply check if the cast would succeed using
. And if for some reason you are
absolutely sure
that a given object can be casted to another object class, there is also
. The latter should be treated similar to
in Swift and only be used if absolutely necessary.
Furthermore,
allows checking for the availability of attributes:

code: let object: LDAPObject<ShadowAccount> // retrieved from a search
object.hasAttribute(\.authPassword) // Returns whether or not `authPassword` is available.

inlineContent.text: Finally,
uses the identifying attribute specified by the object class to conform to
and

abstract.text: A Swift interface for (Open)LDAP.
Represents any object class.
Represents an (typed) attribute containing its key.
Represents a key for an attribute.
Represents a connection to an LDAP server.
Closes (invalidates) this connection.
If this connection is a primary connection (non-duplicate), it will be unbound.
If this is a duplicate connection, it will be destroyed.
Searches the directory for objects of a given object class using the given base and additional filter.
The error type representing errors occurring during LDAP operations.
Represents a concrete object of a given object class type. This type can be used to extract attributes for this object.
Checks whether this object can be casted to the given other object class.
Force casts this object to new object class type.
Represents a raw LDAP value.
Represents a server configuration.
Creates a new server configuration with the given schema, host and port.
Opens a new connection to this server.
Represents a type that can be converted from and to an LDAP raw type.
The base protocol for all object classes.
New object classes should always declare a protocol (MyObjectClassProtocol) and a corresponding implementation (MyObjectClass).
However, the implementation should alway just be an empty struct just containing the oid and the protocol should have all properties defined in an extension.
